speaker: interviewer
text: OK, it's the 30th of July, 1996. The survivor is Joseph Abrahams. The interviewer is Gay Belfer. We are in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia, and this afternoon we'll be speaking English. And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, My name is Gay Belfer, G-A-Y B-E-L-F-E-R, and I'm interviewing today Joseph Abrahams. Today's date is July 30, 1996. The interview is in Sydney, Australia, and the language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say your name, please, and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name, Joseph Abrahams.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-O-S-E-P-H A-B-R-A-H-A-M-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 4th of June, 1907.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just on 89.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And where were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: London.

speaker: interviewer
text: In England?

speaker: interviewee
text: England, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me a little bit about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they came from Lithuania. Two young-- a young boy and a girl. My father was 18. My mother was 17. At 18, he had to join the army. Well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was-- I don't remember now. Harris Abrahams. And my mother's name was-- I've forgotten already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sarah. Sarah. Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah. Sarah Abrahams, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what town did that come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kovno ?] and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And did they speak Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And so you spoke Yiddish at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We born-- born-- we were brought up bilingual. Two languages, Yiddish and English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had five brothers and one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The end-- the very end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And would you like to tell me a little bit about your family and about growing up in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was only a boy, a youngster in London. I went to Jews' Free school-- at the old Jews' Free School, which is a well-known school in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a grade school and a central school. Call it a high school, if you want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me a little bit about your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's not much. I was a baby. My two oldest brothers were in the printing trade. The next one was tailoring. And this-- the fourth one, the fourth brother I had, he was a taxi driver.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister, was she married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, eventually, yes. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And was there a big family with the grandparents or cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plenty of cousins, but grandparents I only knew one, my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- which grandmother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she came from Lithuania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. She lived with the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lived with her daughter [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what-- go ahead. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually when she was older, she went into the Jewish Home in London. It is well known, that home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And do you remember the address where you lived in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- in London, number 8 Ruth House, Flower and Dean street. It's a well-known street. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that in the East End of London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the heart of the East End.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And there were many Jews in that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Practically all Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you had Jewish friends and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. All Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me a little bit more about your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: My school?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews' Free School was a very well-known school. There was about a thousand children then, because at the time children came from Russia from the pogroms. And they all went to the Jewish school. It was free school, because they didn't have to pay. And they came poor-- they-- they came from Russia, poor. The school provided them with clothes. The boys all had suits given to them. Those suits were distinguished. They were brown corduroy, and they had boots, heavy boots that should last long and corduroy suits. And when you saw a boy wear a corduroy suit, you knew he was from Jews' Free School.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what subjects did you learn there? Did-- did you learn Hebrew and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A Jewish school, we had to go to school on Sunday for two hours for Hebrew only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you left that school?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a high school I went to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And-- and what did you plan to do? Did you have plans for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always wanted to be an engineer-- engineering. When I left school, there was no work at all, not even for a boy. It was a real depression then, and I was out of work ages until the people who lived opposite of me, they told my mother that their son's going to start a business on his own. He was in the fur trade, and they're willing to take me in to learn the trade thoroughly. I won't get much-- much pay, but I'll know the trade. In the fur trade, I'll be a fur cutter, which was a high class job. And that's where I finished up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of business was your father in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tailoring. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, and did he work at home, or did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He went out to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And when you were growing up, it was the time of the First World War.

speaker: interviewee
text: First World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Do you-- do you remember that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do. I was only seven when it started, and I remember the air raids when the Zeppelins came over. And my mother was very excitable-- excitable. And as soon as we heard the planes coming over-- airplane-- the airships coming over, the Zeppelins, she took me by the-- the hand, and we rushed to shelter. I remember distinctly. I was only a little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And did any of your brothers fight in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four of them. They were all-- yes. Three came back. One got killed in France about a week before the armistice before the war finished in 1918. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Did that affect the family very much?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother was very upset. And we all had to sit Shiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes. Which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Woolf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you left school, you became a farrier.

speaker: interviewee
text: A farrier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. What else can you tell me about that time in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a fur cutter. I was trained, and I was an expert cutter. And after a time, my employers decided they don't want the factory anymore. We gave-- they're going to give it up. They offered me to take over with one of the brothers-- his brothers, not my brothers-- and we became partners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Where-- where was that factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the East End. No, in the city of London. That's where the fur trade was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Aldersgate street.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you continued to live in the East End?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. That was our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were all your friends Jewish and--

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Did you belong to any organizations or any youth groups or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had no time for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. What was the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to go out and earn a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And was the family Zionistic, and did they talk about Palestine? Did they support-- did they support Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were interested.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not-- not active?

speaker: interviewee
text: But, see, father had to work and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. There was no time for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: No time for it, yes. Unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever go on holidays or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Holdiays? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what sort of places did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the early days of my youth, we used to go locally. Like Brighton-- I'll mention the names, but most people wouldn't know it. In Brighton or South End or so on. When I grew older, we decided to go overseas. So we went to Jersey, the Channel Islands. There, I met my wife. Yes. She was on holiday [? there. ?] Then the next year, we went to-- further [? afield-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: To the continent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Continent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Blankenberge. Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And while you were at school, did you ever learn to speak other languages? Did you learn French or German or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We started to learn German, but I left school by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And during the 1930s when you were a young man, did you hear very much about what was happening in Europe when-- when Hitler came to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard about it, but we didn't know much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you read the newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the people opposite, a relation of theirs came from Germany. He left-- he fled, to put it another way. And they told us so and so was a dentist in Germany, and he had to leave. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were-- they were able to stay in England, those refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just the fella on his own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. .

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And you said you met your wife in Jersey?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. When-- when did you meet her, and when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in 1934. We got engaged 1932, then two years later, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And where were you married? What synagogue was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if you know it, Philpot Street. People who lived in the East End would know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Philpot Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that the same synagogue where you used to go when you were a child, when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We went to a local one. No, that was the big one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And were your parents still alive by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They both died-- well, I got married in March, March the 18th. My mother died in January of that year. My father died in February. Practically together, about five or six weeks difference.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had to get married, and I wanted to cancel the wedding. So we went to the shul. I went to speak to the rabbi. What happens? I'm getting married. Should I cancel the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no. A wedding, you don't-- you don't cancel. So it went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got married without them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- they died from sickness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother had a weak heart. My father had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you got married in 1934, that was the time of the Great Depression. And were you able to still keep working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had my own business [INAUDIBLE]. Worked until the war broke out in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And what about children? Did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born? When?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- that's going back into history now.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was born before the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, during the war. Before the war, yes. That's right. He was about two years when the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And in that time between 1937 when Harold was born and 1939 when the war started, what was happening in England at that time? Was there any talk about Hitler and what was happening to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Talk locally. Not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. And-- and do you remember when the war started in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Too well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Just before the war started, we had an inkling, a good idea that war's gonna breakout. And I said to my wife, I've got to do something. I'll join the civil defense, like locally. If it's going to be air raids, well, I'll help. I went to a local town hall and enlisted. There was no vacancies normally, except in the casualty service. I said, I'll do casualty work. First aid. Started on first aid and war broke out. Then we were called up in the civil defense.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you do that for?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long I did it for? For two years until the air raids eased up a bit, and we had to join the forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's 19-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] '42. No. Would it be?

speaker: interviewee
text: '41. '42, when the air raids eased up. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you joined the civil defense, Joseph, did they train you? What-- what sort of training did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: First aid. First aid and home nursing. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that took up all your time? You weren't able to still-- to still work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When the war started, the fur trade was finished. Everybody fled or left the fur trade. It was a luxury trade, so I was in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And who was in charge of that civil defense? Do you remember who-- who was in charge there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Did you wear a uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: All we wore was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, wore a hat. Yes, we wore a steel helmet. That's right. SB, stretcher bearer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And-- and were there a lot of casualties in that time? Who-- who--

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: In those first two years when you were a volunteer.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a lot of casualties, but actually, we weren't in the heart of the casualty area. Mostly it was in the East End, which was full of people. But we lived on the outskirts, so we didn't have many casualties.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942 came along, and the captain said, all you young blokes go and join the forces. You're no more reserves. I registered in the forces, and they said to me, what work you doing now? I said, at the moment, army casualty service, which was true.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got my calling up papers, Royal Army Medical Corps. I became a medic--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because I was trained.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about that medical corps? Who was in charge of that? Who was your superior officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was immaterial, you know? We went to training, three months' training [INAUDIBLE] exercises, drill and so on. And then we were allocated a unit, which was gonna be our unit. I was allocated to the field ambulance, because I was A1. If you're not A1 in the army in the medical service, they put you in a hospital-- hospital work. Not me. I was in a field ambulance. That is with the infantry, because I was trained and A1.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what does it mean, A1? What does that refer to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fit-- you're fit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you're fit.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's nothing wrong with you. Physically and mentally fit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who were the officers? Were-- were they medical people, the people in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, doctors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Doctors. They have the rank of an officer. Though they're [? only ?] young-- they just got their medical degree-- soon as they became of the medical service, they had a rank officer. We were privates. We'd done all the spade work, but I was-- I was already trained. You would have to go for exams in the army. See, your classification in nursing service. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I had no classification, [? by ?] the first exam I had was NO3, nursing orderly, class three. As you go through the army, you take more exams. You're upgraded. Class two, NO2. Then NO1, which is the highest. That's the highest degree in the nursing service in the army. [INAUDIBLE] if you had the stripes. This is a higher degree. You're trained as a medical nursing,

speaker: interviewer
text: And you passed the exams and you got--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to that level? And-- and how long were you in that service in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. For-- till the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't go higher.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. And where did you do the training? Did they-- did you stay in London or did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no no. A place in Kent called Sittingbourne, Kent, near Chatham. And there we were shifted up to Scotland. We did all our training up in Scotland, because it's rough there. Hills, lakes, and so on, because we were in the casualty service with the infantry. We had to do the same work, same training as infantry, but we weren't allowed to hold a gun. We were protected by the Geneva Convention. We were never allowed to hold a gun, any rifle. So we did the same training, but without arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you wore the army uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Full uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And whereabouts in Scotland were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All over. From the south up to Edinburgh, Glasgow, Inverness, right up north. Then when the-- when the invasion was there, we were shifted over to the west coast. Argyllshire, which is rugged and not many people there. Civilians weren't allowed [? for ?] that part, and there we had to do our training, invasion training.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Landings.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that consist of? What sort of things were they training you to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, going through the water fully clothed. Yes. Doing training in water, climbing up mountains, which is-- Scotland has. That's the whole idea. And we were shifted to the west coast, which is quiet [INAUDIBLE]. And there we were there until the invasion started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only Jew in the-- in the unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my unit, yes. The only one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm coming to that. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you treated in any way differently, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All the [? thoughts ?] were good, except one. A newcomer came along and he-- they soon shifted him out. But all the others weren't anti-Semitic. They were very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Did they give you any time for the Jewish holidays, or did they make provision for kosher food, anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kosher food-- I had kosher food sent to me from London. I registered as I don't eat non-kosher, and this organization in London then they sent [INAUDIBLE] and tin stuff from London.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --survive on that.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. What about the holidays and the Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had it at the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish boys got together in a different unit, and they had-- well, a make-believe service, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was there a rabbi or a Jewish chaplain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but not for us only, for the area. His name--

speaker: interviewer
text: Harding? Harding? Was it Reverend Harding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harding, yes. Later on became the Rabbi Harding. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he from London or was he from Scotland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he conducted the services and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, only special ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you said there was some incident of anti-Semitism. Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: The incident where somebody who was anti-Semitic in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the unit. Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't there. I was told about it. And he was speaking about a Jew boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was talking about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: About me. I didn't hear it, but we had a lot of Scots in our unit. And when they heard about this, they set about him. Don't you ever say that again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they defended you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, I was a good-- a good [? turn to ?] them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in charge of your unit? Do you remember who was in charge of your unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: The officer?

speaker: interviewer
text: The commanding officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dixon. Major Dixon. D-I-X-O-N, Dixon.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a doctor too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And so how long were you in Scotland? How long were you training there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five or six weeks then the invasion started, and we were shifted straight down to Portsmouth, the south-- south of England. And there the landing craft was waiting-- waiting, and we were put on board, ready for invasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And that was-- that was D-Day, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-Day, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that when you arrived in-- in Normandy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Actually, I was in the field unit-- we weren't wanted until a couple of days afterwards. But we were at sea in the landing craft waiting to be called in. And when we were wanted, then they brought us in. On the beaches, land in the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people in the group when you landed on the beaches? How many--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I can't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. No. And what happened when you when you came to Normandy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we landed on the beaches. Not on the sand, in the water. Waded through, got onto sand, and then we went onto land. We were still being bombed. German planes were still about, and we had to go for cover. We went into-- scampered to the nearest field, and we laid down by the hedge. There was a hedge there. And we saw this plane coming over. We thought it was one of ours. Well, we were watching. We-- suddenly we saw him diving down towards us and machine gunning. He drove along this hedge, machine gunning the hedge lengthwise. Fortunately, the bullets landed about six inches away from me. I could see in the soil the bullets going there. It just bit wide for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any casualties?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm trying to think [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape one of the interview with Joseph Abrahams. It's the 30th of July, 1996, and we're in Sydney, Australia.
